turns.content: I'm reviewing a colleague's code today and I'm confused about the difference between flat_map and flatten. My manager said we should use flat_map for flattening nested iterables, but I thought that was just what flatten does.

That is a common confusion because they look similar in result but work differently under the hood. Flatten takes an iterator of iterators and yields every item from the inner ones without changing values, while flat_map applies a function to each element before flattening. If you have Option::Result pairs inside another collection, flatten just extracts the values ignoring the wrapper type, whereas flat_map can transform those values as well.
Got it so far but I made a wrong assumption earlier thinking that since both produce the same output order flat_map is redundant. I just want to flatten everything regardless of what's inside.

You are right about the final sequence when no transformation happens, but flat_map can also filter or modify items before flattening them which makes it more flexible than plain flatten. For example if you have a Result where some values might need to be converted from String to u32 before being added to the output stream then flat_map handles that conversion while flatten would leave the Strings untouched.
Okay so in my current code I have a Vec of Option<String> and I want to convert each string into u32 before flattening. Should I use flat_map here?

Yes exactly you should use flat_map because it applies your conversion function to each element before flattening the results. You would call map on the Option<String> iterator transforming each Some value into u32 and then flatten to get a Vec<u32>. Using plain flatten here would keep the Strings as-is which is not what you want.
Wait but I was told that flat_map only works with iterators that produce one element per iteration. What happens if my inner iterator produces multiple elements?

That is a misunderstanding of how the trait works. The map function in flat_map applies to each input element and returns exactly one output element regardless of what you pass inside the closure unless you explicitly handle multiple outputs through additional logic like using collect or chaining more iterators.
So if I wanted to expand a Vec of Vecs where each inner vec has 2 elements I can't just use flat_map?
You don't need special logic for that case either. Just apply map over the outer iterator and flatten will handle expanding all inner vectors into a single flat sequence automatically.

I'm trying to understand how the iterator syntax works when I have nested data, specifically with Option or Result types. How does this connect to what I know about map functions in functional programming?

You are spot on connecting this to map because flat_map is essentially a chain where you apply a function that returns an iterable instead of just a single value. The difference is that while a standard map transforms items into one item, flat_map takes the output of your closure and flattens it immediately if it's already an iterator or Option/Result. For example, when processing a list of Option<T>, using flat_map lets you filter out None values before moving to the next step without needing multiple passes.
Okay so I was thinking I needed to call flatten separately after map. Is that correct, or is there a misunderstanding in how these two traits interact?

You have hit on a common misconception; flat_map does not require a separate flatten call because it combines the mapping and flattening logic into one operation. The trait impl for Iterator provides this method specifically to handle cases where your closure returns an iterator, Option, or Result, so you don't need to chain map followed by flatten manually. This keeps the code more concise and avoids unnecessary intermediate collections.
